Narrative_Meaning: Fours signify stability and foundations. They usually indicate some foundation, whether an organization or a building, that may have its foundation weakened, damaged or strengthened by the narrative.

Yarn.Yarn_Description: The Tower card can represent physical buildings, places, and stability. Less commonly, in the form a Wizard's or Watchtower it may represent research, viewing, observation or looking-out. The Tower is a stable landmark, not prone to collapse, except when struck by lightning, which happens often before the lightning conductor, or earthquake, which is considered an act of the gods before the modern day also. The card has a hint of divine retribution about this meaning, which is why it may be called the Tower Struck By Lightning in negative positions.

Significator.Plot: The Tower Plot is one that builds up piece by piece. Such a Plot doesn't begin as a Cycle or Epic, it starts small, with a Single Story. The Plot will return, each time promoted, over and over until it dominates the whole Story. Such Plots reflect the emergence of new cultures, new technologies and new societies, it is also useful in world-building as each Story builds upon the last occurrence until the new world is defined.
Significator.Side: When a side is marked with the Tower then they are in a period of construction, getting more powerful and preparing for something beyond this immediate conflict, perhaps a later or larger Plot that needs foreshadowing. Their Embodiments are usually less engaged in the Plot (unless Dominant) than normal because of this work.
Significator.Character:
The Tower Significator indicates a Character who is a prominent figure of their society.

They Gain Yin when they speak calmly, suggest an alternative option, or prioritize problems.

They Gain Yang when they keep silent, agree with another or organize assistance.

Gain Chi when they demonstrate physical prowess.

They gain +1 Success Levels when they perform physical actions (firing a gun doesn't count).
Significator.Scene: The Tower Scene usually represents a Construction or Research Ordeal, often about or to "purchase" a Location. It can also create a Force-of-Nature Extra, as an Earthquake or Storm to attack a building. Yarn-Tellers can play the Tower to Gain (or build) a Location, research a topic, or observe a Location, or perhaps unleash a Storm or Earthquake.

Yarn.Aspect_Description: The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.
Yarn.Hook_Description: The Story opens with a Lookout or Scout bringing news, of an impending threat, this might be "Land Ho!", or "Smoke!" or "Three Thousand Orcs and Ogres are marching South along the King's road. They will be here in two days, maybe less." or the PCs themselves may be the one's to see the threat, and have to raise the alarm.
Yarn.Gain: Location (often Real Estate or a Province where appropriate)
Yarn.Fray_Description: A terrible, traumatic, life changing, paradigm shifting event. Usually a disaster of this magnitude is a Force of Nature Extra (at least), in an Ordeal that has High-Paradoxical Stakes. It is common for new Hitches to result from this Fray, ideally that will deepen the hooks of the Conflict in the PCs, perhaps even causing them to internalise the actual Conflict within themselves.
Yarn.Snag_Description: The Fray ends / leads to a situation where the Embodiment must hold position against a force of lesser opponents (usually Vex and Chorus Extras) that outnumber the Embodiment and Allies. Usually this Snag Ordeal will have a limited duration, with either reinforcements arriving, or some extraction team en-route, or perhaps the enemies will stop with dawn's light, or at nightfall. Otherwise, the Vexes will come in waves, with rest periods in between.

NPC.Description: The Privileged are the high-born, indolent class, who live in fine towers. They live lives of boredom and often corruption, although some may find other hobbies and activities.

Yarn.Sweeping_Text: The PCs have a stable situation for a while (this may be weeks or months)
Yarn.History_Description: The Tower indicates that the current situation has been built, brick by brick, as one might construct a tower. This situation was deliberately engineered by someone for a specific purpose.
Yarn.Situation_Description: The Tower indicates that the current situation is essentially stable, sometimes this means if you try to change it, something will happen to stabilise it once again, other times it just means that it would be very difficult to change anything.
Yarn.Solution_Description: The Tower indicates that the solution to the current situation is to watch or look over the situation carefully. This could be a stakeout, observation, or research about the situation, that is itself the solution, rather than revealing a solution.

Revelations.About_Description: The information revealed is about the Internal Conflict Embodiment. The Internal side of a Conflict may be within all sides or just one. It might be traitors, moles, spies, spirits or just a Character's internal conflict.
Revelations.Info_Description: The available information points to a number of potential conclusions. Usually only one of them is true.
Revelations.Vector_Description: A Hallucination that reveals information is common enough that it gets a mention. Of course, what we don't hear about are all the times hallucinations aren\t useful.
Revelations.Alternate_Description: The information is about a place, or time where either Embodiment will feel, or be safe. If you need a safe space, this tells you where to find one, if you have a safe space, it tells you where your Opposed Embodiment feels safe.
Revelations.Detail_Description: A Legal detail can be a point of law, a loophole, or miscarriage of justice that can be exploited somehow. You may not have the villain's address, but thanks to this restraining order you know who his lawyers are...

Obstacle.Description: The way is blocked by a building, wall or tower. There may be guards, locked doors, or similar, but you have to go through, around, or over this obstacle.

Tarot.Crossed: +1 Success Level on any social action or Psych attack.
Tarot.Normal: +1 Failure Level of any social action or Psych attack.

Ordeal.Stage_Description: The way ahead is slick, perhaps a recently polished floor, or something has been spilt, like oil. Or maybe the ground is icy.
Ordeal.Motional_Description: They're right on top of us, take the wheel and keep down, I need to shoot back. Oh, try not to crash, yeah?
Ordeal.Tide_of_Battle_Text: The Flowing side has captured a defensible position or acquired some tactical advantage. If they can hold this position for this Stage they will secure their momentum into the next Round. Failure to hold this position however will result in the Tide reversing.
Ordeal.Fight_Description:
The Fight is a Stealth mission, to penetrate a secure facility, achieve some goal (often an assassination or theft) and then escape. Opponents are usually Cast Extras (security guards) although they may be Chorus Monsters.
  Facet    Monster  
Gossamer ⚊ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Description: The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. Competition, perception, endurance and curiosity are positive aspects associated with the suit, but spades also represent destruction, betrayal, distance, might and death. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.

Tarot Meaning: Misfortune, Suffering, Loss, Mourning, Treachery, Betrayal, War, Lawsuit, Conflict.

Ordeal Text: Ordeal Types: Confrontation (e.g. Warfare, Lawsuit), Competition (Sports, Quizzes etc), Perception (Spot Hidden). Spades act as Trumps for the Following Action Types: Attacking, Balance, Climbing, Contagion, Desecration, Destruction, Feats of Strength, Lock-picking, Navigation, Perception (Search/Spot), Staying power, and Stoicism. Spades can be used for any type of attack.

Parry:Redirect-AttackThe damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.

Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Wiry — The Monster is immensely physically powerful, although often its form may not reveal it. It often demonstrates supernatural power as well as strength and toughness. A small, strong Goblin, a robot that looks like a little girl, a cybernetically enhanced rat, or even a killer doll (when not an Object Monster) are all examples of a Gossamer Monster. The Wiry Monster can add Gossamer Score cards to its Monster Pool whenever it is underestimated, cared for, or demonstrates its strength. A Wiry monster may add their current Monster Pool Size Score (Number Reduced) Success Levels to any action with their strength or toughness. Wiries must spend cards from their Monster Pool as though it was a Prepared Defence to ignore damage.
Inertia ⚋ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.

Spades can be used for any type of attack.

Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Unstoppable — The Monster is inexorable, it does not stop and cannot be killed by any normal means. They start play with a Monster Pool of Inertia Score cards. Unstoppable Monsters can add Inertia Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they cross a threshold, pass a barrier, or are forced to stop or turn around. Unstoppable Monsters can ignore a number of Wounds equal to the number of cards in their Monster Pool. Wounds stacked beyond the Monster Pool affect them as normal. Inexorable Monsters can also ignore all Hitches with a Boon less than their Monster Pool Size. Whenever an Unstoppable Monster is damaged beyond this limit, it must Discard the Highest Card in the Monster Pool, it may immediately also Discard any Wounds lower than discarded Monster Card, any remaining Wounds are then discarded on a one Monster Pool card per Wound basis. However, while discarding these wounds and cards the Monster must remain still (playing dead) until a Chi Extend Duration equal to the Number of Monster Pool cards Discarded (the initial card is instant, the next discard takes an action, then a second takes a few seconds, the third Wound takes an Ordeal Round, 25 Wounds would take a week). Examples of the Inertial Monster type include the Hungry Dead (Zombies) and most robotic assassins (especially ones from the future).
Key ⚊ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Hunter — The Monster is a hunter or tracker, it should favour harrying attacks and hit and run tactics. Typically such a Monster will vanish or flee if it is wounded, only to reappear when healed. The hunter may be fooled by false trails, but never for long. Hunters may add Key Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they spot a track, clue, or similar, that indicates the subject of their hunt. Hunters receive a bonus to their Score or Pips equal to the number of cards in the Monster Pool on any tracking, attacking or hunting actions if they also spend a card from the Monster Pool on the action. Hunters may convert Mortal and Carnage Wounds that they take into Crippling Wounds instead, at a cost of one card from their Monster Pool. In T13, the archetypal version of this Monster is a Bulmäs Pack member from the Wild Hunt, but any Werewolf or Animal Shifter would probably qualify, as would robot cowboys in theme parks that have gone wrong, and any fantasy world’s Elven ranger.
Miasma ⚋ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Description: The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.

Spades can be used for any type of attack.

Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Mutant — The monster is a mutant or chimerical creature made from parts of others. This monster type is the oldest perhaps, you’ll find examples throughout the ancient world, particularly in Greek myth. The Mutant will often be larger, and may be malformed or unsymmetrical, with odd numbers of limbs, eyes, horns, or even heads. The Chimera will blend multiple animals together to create a new creature that combines features of all the “parent” creatures, preserving the best of all species. Mutant Monsters start with Miasma Score Monster Pool. They may add Miasma Draw cards to the Pool every time they accomplish a specified goal, such as eating a human, consuming a soul, bathing in radiation, or just growing a year older, etc. The Monster Pool can be used as a “Mutation” Annex, which can be used to mimic the abilities of the Mutants “parent” creatures, such as a spider’s ability to climb, or an eagle’s ability to fly. The Mutation Annex has a Boon equal to the Value of the Number of Cards in the Monster Pool, and so draws cards equal to the Monster Pool Size Reduced and plays a number of cards equal to the Miasma Draw. The Mutation Annex is often used as a Size Annex for the Mutant. Where a 5 card Monster Pool would create a Boon 16 Annex, and could grant a Kaiju Mutant the size of a Skyscraper. 1950s B-movie monsters are often of this type, giant ants, lizards, spiders, wasps, and even rabbits have been used. Classically of course, Cyclopses, Centaurs, Minotaurs, Satyrs, Gryphons (or Griffins), Hippogriffs, Dragons, Sphinxes, Manticores and of course the Chimera itself are all examples. You can also find examples in superheroes, especially those created by radioactive arachnids, or those with an X-gene.
Trial ⚊ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Soak: Counter-AttackThe matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate.

Killer — The Killer Monster is a deadly Combat specialist of some sort. Killers add Trial Draw cards to their Monster Pool each time they kill. Killers automatically add +1 Success Level per Monster Pool Size Score (Number Reduced) to all attacks, and Monster Pool Size Score (Number Reduced) is automatically added to the Pips of any attack as Penetration. Killers must spend Monster Pool cards whenever they will make a Trick either with other cards being played by the Ordeal Pool, or other cards in the Round Pool or a Style Reserve. Killers can be drug-addled assassins, crazed shooters, or just steel-souled soldiers.
Yonder ⚋ ♠

Suit: Spades ♠

Ordeal Trump: Attack / Perception / Penetration (Opening / Navigation / Logic)

Description: The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. Alien — The Monster is an alien, an outsider. It may just be a metaphor for alienation, or literally be an extra-(or ultra)-terrestrial. Such monsters are usually obvious and inhuman, not just to look at, but in the way they think and behave. They may have apparently supernatural powers and abilities, but often make do with advanced technologies (in the form of additional Descendants). They may add Yonder Draw cards to their Monster Pool when they are observed doing their alien / inhuman / weird actions. They may add Monster Pool Size Reduced Success Levels to any action to do with their alien abilities, technologies, motion or survival (including Dodge, Soak, etc). Alien Monsters must play cards from their Monster Pool to socialise with “non-aliens”. From little grey Eusaiveans, through pod people and pepper-pot shaped engines of hate, all the way to face-hugging space-wasps, there are plenty of aliens.
  Facet    Monster

Lea.Notes: Tiny Arthropods usually with biting mouth parts

Stress.Description: Coping mechanisms for stress vary from person to person. Some people will talk about what is stressing them, creating a new narrative out of the situation at had, others will let stress get the better of them, corrupting them, or driving them to attack, others will hide themselves away, and very few may lose senses.  These coping mechanisms can grow rapidly becoming Flaws or even Woes as a Character relies on them to cope with Stress.
Stress.Rules: Coping Characters create a new Binding, Laments, Confined, Corrupted, Offensive or Loss-of-Sense Quirk automatically initially with a Bane of 1. Characters may move Stress into this Hitch, increasing the Banes by 1 for the current Banes in Stress any time the Hitch is Triggered.

Trauma.Description: Trauma can lead a Character to seeking safe spaces, building panic rooms, buying and installing security systems, and generally developing vigilance against future Traumas.
Trauma.Rules: The Trauma causes a Character to feel physically unsafe for a majority of their time. It immediately Relieves all current Stress, but creates an "Unsafe" Pool. They may seek to purchase armour, secure Locations and the like, of a value at least equal to the "Unsafe" Pool, as this will reduce the Pool by 1 each time. They may also Relieve 1 Stress gained later by checking every potential entrance and exit is secured.

Age.Description: An Age of Expansion is occurring. During this age a society will seek out new territories and locations, and construct and build upon this new land they have acquired. Societies in this age will often build an impressive building or buildings that will become historically significant with time, such as churches, cathedrals, tithe barns, exchanges and official market buildings, banks, palaces, henges, castles, citadels, town walls, town halls, public baths, court buildings, etc.
Age.Rules: During an Age of Expansion a society and the individual members will undertake various Ordeals to capture, colonise, explore, find, gain, purchase or rent a new location, and convert that location to their purposes, often by constructing infrastructure and buildings in the new Location. Such a society-wide Ordeal is similar to an Age of Endeavour, where the society works for a common goal, in this case the common goal is less common to all members of the society, but the society will benefit from the expansion as a whole.
